Article.ArticleTitle: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Aztreonam-Avibactam Compared with Colistin Plus Meropenem in Treating Metallo-Beta-Lactamase-Producing Enterobacterales Infections in Spain.

Abstract.AbstractText: Aztreonam-avibactam (ATM-AVI) is the first β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor antibiotic for treating serious infections caused by multidrug-resistant Gram-negative bacteria, including metallo-β-lactamases (MBL)-Enterobacterales. The objective of this analysis was to analyze the efficiency of ATM-AVI versus colistin + meropenem (COL+MER) in adult patients with complicated intra-abdominal infections (cIAI) or hospital-acquired pneumonia/ventilator-associated pneumonia (HAP/VAP) based on the REVISIT study.

A model combining a decision tree and a Markov model has been adapted to the Spanish National Healthcare System perspective, considering a lifetime horizon. Patients' characteristics, clinical (probabilities of resistance, cure, mortality, recurrence, nephrotoxicity), utility, and economic inputs (direct medical costs) have been obtained from the REVISIT study and published evidence.

It was found that ATM-AVI is a cost-effective alternative compared to COL+MER with an incremental cost-utility ratio (ICUR) of €3116.2 per quality-adjusted life year (QALY) gained, derived of an incremental gain of 0.6 QALYs and an additional cost of €1875.9. For each indication, an ICUR of €1284.1/QALY-gained for cIAI and €4198.6/QALY-gained for HAP/VAP were obtained. Moreover, sensitivity analysis showed that ATM-AVI would be dominant in 14.5% of cases.
ATM-AVI is a highly cost-effective antibiotic versus COL+MER in the management of patients with serious infections caused by MBL-Enterobacterales.

Clinical Microbiology Section, Ramón y Cajal-IRYCIS University Hospital, CIBERINFEC, Instituto de Salud Carlos III, Madrid, Spain.
Hospital Pharmacy Service, Hospital Universitari Son Espases, Palma, Spain.
Biomedical Research Network Center of Infectious Diseases (CIBERINFEC), Institute of Health Carlos III, Madrid, Spain.
Biomedical Research Network Center of Infectious Diseases (CIBERINFEC), Institute of Health Carlos III, Madrid, Spain. julian.torre.sspa@juntadeandalucia.es.
Infectious Diseases Service, Reina Sofía University Hospital, Córdoba, Spain. julian.torre.sspa@juntadeandalucia.es.
Maimonides Institute of Biomedical Research of Córdoba (IMIBIC), Córdoba, Spain. julian.torre.sspa@juntadeandalucia.es.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Conflict of interest: Gerardo Aguilar received financial support for speaking at meetings organized on behalf of Shionogi, Gilead, Merck Sharp and Dohme (MSD), Mundipharma, Pfizer, as well as unrestricted research grants from Shionogi, MSD and Pfizer. Patricia Ruiz-Garbajosa has received honoraria from Advanz and Viatris Pharmaceuticals for consulting and from Pfizer, Shionogy, MSD and Menarini for training activities. Leonor Periañez Parraga has received consulting honoraria from Advanz and MSD. Training activities have been funded by Pfizer, Shionogy, MSD, Menarini, and Advanz. María Maroto-Díaz, María del Carmen de Ceano-Vivas, Alfonso De Lossada Juste, Carlota Moya-Alarcón and Maria Gheorghe are employees of Pfizer and may hold Pfizer stock. Alba Villacampa Lordan and Sara Arranz Gonzalo are employees of Hygeia Consulting. Julián Torre-Cisneros has received consulting honoraria from Shionogy, Advanz and MSD. Training activities have been funded by Pfizer, Shionogy, MSD, Menarini, and Advanz. Ethical Approval: This article is based on previously conducted studies and does not contain any new studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
